parameters.text: =✅ Capture API callbacks with Notion 24/7 — run completed at {{ $now.toFormat('HH:mm') }}

parameters.content:
### Receive Inbound Webhook

Entry point: listens for POST calls on `/wf06068-job`. Use the TEST url while building, switch callers to the PRODUCTION url after publishing.

Next: Read Pending Queue (Notion)

### Read Pending Queue (Notion)

Pulls every page from the Notion database — fine for small datasets; add a filter if the database grows large.

Ports: ①→ Normalize Job Payload · ②→ Capture Failure Context

↻ retries 3×/2s · ⚠ failures exit via the error rail · 🔑 Notion integration token

### Normalize Job Payload

Custom JavaScript: Normalize incoming job records to a stable schema

Next: Work in Batches

### Work in Batches

Loop controller — releases 20 item(s) per pass. Output 2 (loop) processes the batch, output 1 (done) fires after the last batch.

Ports: ①→ Aggregate Batch Results · ②→ Process Batch Item

### Process Batch Item

POST https://api.example.com/v1/jobs — retries 3× with backoff on failure.

Ports: ①→ Respect Rate Limits · ②→ Capture Failure Context

↻ retries 3×/2s · ⚠ failures exit via the error rail · 🔑 API key / bearer for the target API

### Respect Rate Limits

Pauses 2 seconds — spacing calls to respect rate limits.

Next: Work in Batches

### Aggregate Batch Results

Collapses all items into a single summary item (for counts and run stats).

Next: Mark Completed in Google Sheets

### Mark Completed in Google Sheets

Upserts a row into 'Job Tracker', matched on `jobId` — re-running with the same key updates the row instead of duplicating it.

Ports: ①→ Align Field Names · ②→ Capture Failure Context

↻ retries 3×/2s · ⚠ failures exit via the error rail · 🔑 Google OAuth2 / service account

nodes.name: Note: Mark Completed in Google Sheets

parameters.content:
### Align Field Names

Normalizes field names so every source feeds the same downstream schema.

Next: Cap Batch Volume

### Cap Batch Volume

Caps the stream at 25 items to protect downstream quotas.

Next: Sort Jobs Newest First

### Sort Jobs Newest First

Orders items (newest first) so limits and digests pick the most relevant records.

Next: Send Summary to Slack

### Send Summary to Slack

Posts a message to Slack (`#automation`) — a low-latency way for the team to see automation activity without checking a dashboard.

↻ retries 3×/2s · ⚠ failures exit via the error rail · 🔑 Slack OAuth2

### Capture Failure Context

Shapes the item: writes `failedNode`, `errorMessage`, `workflow`. Keeps the schema explicit between stages.

Next: Escalate Failure to Slack

### Escalate Failure to Slack

Posts a message to Slack (`#automation`) — a low-latency way for the team to see automation activity without checking a dashboard.

↻ retries 3×/2s · 🔑 Slack OAuth2

# Capture API callbacks with Notion 24/7

**Pattern:** Webhook Pipeline · **Level:** Core · **Trigger:** Webhook

## What it does
Capture API callbacks with Notion 24/7. It fires the moment an external system calls its webhook.

## Why it exists
Manual process orchestration is slow and error-prone; this workflow makes the process repeatable, monitored and hands-off, with failures surfaced instead of silently lost.

## Main flow
Receive Inbound Webhook → Read Pending Queue (Notion) → Normalize Job Payload → Work in Batches → Aggregate Batch Results → Mark Completed in Google Sheets → Align Field Names → …

## Use it for
- Teams that want to webhook pipeline without manual effort
- Anyone automating process orchestration in productivity
- Replacing a fragile manual process with a reliable workflow

## You get
- Removes copy-paste errors
- Standardizes the process every time
- Keeps your systems in sync

## Before first run
Connect credentials: API key / bearer for the target API; Google OAuth2 / service account; Notion integration token; Slack OAuth2.
Follow the color key: 🟦 entry · 🟪 AI · 🟫 actions · ⬜ utilities · 🟩 outputs · 🟥 error rail.
Each node has its own doc card underneath — read those before editing.

## Try it now
Sample data is pinned on the trigger — hit **Execute Workflow** to watch it flow through every node before connecting any real credentials. Unpin the trigger (right-click → Unpin) when you're ready to go live.

## Design decisions

The batch size and the Wait between iterations exist specifically to respect the downstream API's rate limit — processing everything as fast as possible just gets the workflow throttled or banned. Marking items completed only after a batch succeeds leaves a clean resume point if the run dies mid-way, instead of an ambiguous half-processed state.

## Practice exercises

Once it's wired up with real credentials, try:

1. Lower the batch size and raise the wait — measure the throughput/reliability tradeoff directly.
2. Simulate a failed item mid-batch and see exactly what 'Mark Completed' captures.
3. Point the queue read at a different store platform.

## 🛟 Error handling & retries

External calls retry **3× with 2s backoff**. Anything still failing exits through its node's error output into the red error rail → context is captured and the on-call channel is alerted. Set an n8n **Error Workflow** in settings as the final safety net.

## ⏱ Rate limiting

Work is batched and spaced with Wait so provider quotas aren't tripped. If you hit 429s, lower the batch size or raise the wait — throughput drops, reliability rises.

## 🔐 Security

Never trust inbound payloads: the webhook path is unguessable. Add header auth or IP allow-listing for defense in depth. Secrets live in n8n credentials — never in node parameters.

## 🛠 Customize me

Quick wins: point the data nodes at your own accounts (see 🔑 lines on each card), adjust the trigger cadence, and tune thresholds/labels to your process orchestration process. Every node card explains its role — safe to extend at any marked branch.
